query:
You are an expert fact-checker. You are provided with a claim and related evidence. Based only on the provided evidence, determine if the given claim is either supported, refuted, has conflicting evidence, or has not enough evidence to determine its veracity. Write a single sentence that states your verdict and the main reason for it, referencing and synthesizing the most relevant pieces of evidence that support your verdict. Do not copy sentences from the evidence verbatim. Always paraphrase and synthesize the evidence in your own words.

Claim: President Donald Trump always knew that the Coronavirus was a pandemic.
prediction: <statement>Based on the evidence provided, the claim that President Donald Trump always knew that the Coronavirus was a pandemic is not supported. The evidence shows that Trump initially downplayed the risks of the virus and did not take aggressive measures to slow its spread. He also made false claims about the situation improving and blamed others for the virus.<cite>[1-1][3-3][4-8]</cite></statement>
claim: President Donald Trump always knew that the Coronavirus was a pandemic.
label: Refuted
justification: Trump has downplayed the coronavirus, showing he didn't always know it was a pandemic.
decomposed_model_answer: The claim that President Donald Trump always knew that the Coronavirus was a pandemic is not supported.
Trump initially downplayed the risks of the Coronavirus.
Trump did not take aggressive measures to slow the spread of the Coronavirus.
Trump made false claims about the Coronavirus situation improving.
Trump blamed others for the Coronavirus.

statements.statement: Based on the evidence provided, the claim that President Donald Trump always knew that the Coronavirus was a pandemic is not supported. The evidence shows that Trump initially downplayed the risks of the virus and did not take aggressive measures to slow its spread. He also made false claims about the situation improving and blamed others for the virus.

citation.cite: He could have taken aggressive measures to slow the spread of the virus.
citation.pre_context:
China was starting to take drastic measures and was on the verge of closing off the city of Wuhan.
In the weeks that followed, Trump faced a series of choices.
citation.post_context: He could have insisted that the United States ramp up efforts to produce test kits. He could have emphasized the risks that the virus presented and urged Americans to take precautions if they had reason to believe they were sick.

citation.cite: Blaming foreigners and, anachronistically, the Obama administration. Claiming incorrectly that the situation was improving.
citation.pre_context: Trump’s priorities were different: Making the virus sound like a minor nuisance. Exaggerating his administration’s response.
citation.post_context: Trying to cheer up stock market investors. (It was fitting that his first public comments were from Davos and on CNBC.)

citation.cite: Throughout late February, Trump also continued to claim the situation was improving.
citation.pre_context: The next week, Trump would blame an Obama administration regulation for slowing the production of test kits. There was no truth to the charge.
citation.post_context: On Feb. 26, he said: “We’re going down, not up.
